coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive “off-on” sensor based on MXene and magnetic microspheres for simultaneous detection of lung cancer biomarkers - Neuron specific enolase and carcinoembryonic antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chenyu
Zhao, Xiang
Liu, Zhizhou
Ma, Le
Sun, Pengyun
He, Liang
Huang, Huajie
Bai, Pengli
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a highly sensitive lung cancer biomarkers detection probe was developed based on Ag and MXene co-functionalized magnetic microspheres. By using carboxyl magnetic microspheres as carrier, MXene was coated repeatedly by Poly (allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) as interlayer adhesive, and silver particles grown on the surface of MXene in situ can efficiently improve the sensitivity of the probe. The detection of neuron specific enolase (NSE) is mainly through the formation of a specific complex between NSE antigen and antibody, and the release of antibody labeled with amino carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the surface of Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs), so that the fluorescence is restored and "OFF-ON" is formed. The biosensor exhibits excellently wide linear range (0.0001–1500 ng/mL) and the limit of detection (LOD) is up to 0.03 pg/mL, which is superior to most tumor marker probes based on fluorescence mechanism. Furthermore, we constructed dual detection strategy for NSE and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) simultaneously.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MXene
Microspheres
Ag nanoparticles
High sensitivity
Neuron specific enolase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In suit synthesis of Ag@MXene@MCMs composite
Analytical performance of the anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs based fluorescent biosensor
Dual biosensors for simultaneous detection of NSE and CEA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youth Foundation of Shandong Natural Science Foundation
Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China (Grant No. 2022YFC2406600), Youth Foundation of Shandong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. ZR2020QB110), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association, the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. 2019321).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs biosensor for the fluorometric NSE detection.

SEM image of MCMs (A), MXene@MCMs (B), Ag@MXene@MCMs (C). EDS scans the selected single microsphere (D), stacking of multi-layer spectra of different elements (E). EDS mapping images of C (F), Ti (G), Ag (H), and O (I) of Ag@MXene@MCMs.

(A) Diffraction pattern of MCMs, MXene, MXene@MCMs, Ag@MXene@MCMs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of MXene, AgNO3, Ag@MXene@MCMs. (C) Magnetization curve of MCMs and Ag@MXene@MCMs. (D) Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of MCMs, MXene@MCMs, Ag@MXene@MCMs.

(A) Selection of proper excitation wavelength of CQDs. (B) Selection of buffer for EDC/NHS reaction. (C) Selection of proper temperature for EDC/NHS reaction. (D) Fluorescence value after different washing times. (E) Fluorescence recovery intensity of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCM prepared at different time was measured. (F) The fluorescence intensity was measured after the NSE antigen (1 ng/mL) with different incubation times.

(A) Analytical performance of the anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs. (B) Graph between recovered fluorescence and different NSE concentration (0.0001–1500 ng/mL). (C) Linear fitting of different NSE concentration detection by anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs. (D) Analytical performance of the anti-NSE/CQDs/MXene@MCMs based fluorescent biosensor toward NSE detection. (E) Graph between recovered fluorescence and different NSE concentration (0.0001–1500 ng/mL). (F) Linear fitting of different NSE concentration detection by anti-NSE/CQDs/MXene@MCMs.
(A) Histogram of NSE specific detection. (B) Histogram of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm.

(A) The fluorescence recovery after the addition of NSE antigen and CEA antigen simultaneously. (B) The linear fitting of fluorescence intensity and LOG concentration of NSE (blue) and CEA (red). red plots represent CEA and the blue plots represent NSE.

(A) The concentration of NSE and CEA antigens decreased gradually (10–150 ng/mL). (B) The linear fitting curve of theoretical value and measured value at different antigen concentrations (10–150 ng/mL).
Simultaneous determination of NSE and CEA in normal serum.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive “off-on” sensor based on MXene and magnetic microspheres for simultaneous detection of lung cancer biomarkers - Neuron specific enolase and carcinoembryonic antigen

Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chenyu Yin and Xiang Zhao are contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a highly sensitive lung cancer biomarkers detection probe was developed based on Ag and MXene co-functionalized magnetic microspheres. By using carboxyl magnetic microspheres as carrier, MXene was coated repeatedly by Poly (allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) as interlayer adhesive, and silver particles grown on the surface of MXene in situ can efficiently improve the sensitivity of the probe. The detection of neuron specific enolase (NSE) is mainly through the formation of a specific complex between NSE antigen and antibody, and the release of antibody labeled with amino carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the surface of Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs), so that the fluorescence is restored and "OFF-ON" is formed. The biosensor exhibits excellently wide linear range (0.0001–1500 ng/mL) and the limit of detection (LOD) is up to 0.03 pg/mL, which is superior to most tumor marker probes based on fluorescence mechanism. Furthermore, we constructed dual detection strategy for NSE and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) simultaneously.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As people's focus on their health increases, greater demands are made on the identification of different tumor markers [1,2]. It is possible to differentiate between small cell lung cancer (SCLC) and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) using the sensitive biomarker of NSE. Lung cancer indicators such as NSE and CEA have been applied in therapeutic settings. Nowadays, chemiluminescence immunoassay (CLIA) [3],enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4],suspension array [5], fluorescence immunoassay [6], and are just a few of the numerous techniques used for biomarker identification. These conventional approaches do have certain drawbacks, though, like limited sensitivity and time consumption.

To overcome the above limitations, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism to construct tumor marker probes has been burgeoning due to its high efficiency and sensitivity. Graphene is used as common energy receptor [7]. The electrical biosensors composed of nickel ferrocyanide nanoparticles (NiHCFNPs) and gold functionalized graphene sheets were developed to detect NSE, and the final detection limit could reach 0.3 pg/mL. Functionalized graphene quantum dots were used as energy donors and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as energy acceptors to preparation fluorescence-turned-on biosensor for NSE, and the detection limit was up to 0.09 pg/mL [8].

Due to its performance being similar to that of graphene [9], MXene, a novel type of two-dimensional (2D) material, is now being employed as an energy acceptor material [10]. For the purpose of identifying tumor markers, various FRET platforms based on MXene were created [11]. The FRET mechanism can be briefly described as: the excited fluorophore (donor) transfers the excited static energy to a light absorption molecule (acceptor). This energy transfer is non-radioactive, and the main reason is the dipole-dipole interaction between donor and acceptor. Through dipole reaction, a donor in an excited state transfers energy to a nearby acceptor in a non-excited way. The primary obstacle to the continued advancement of MXene-based fluorescence biosensors, however, is the fluorescence background. Ultrasensitive biosensors were created by conjugating Ag or Au nanoparticles with MXenes to increase sensitivity [12–14]. Due to its low background, MXene application significantly increases detection sensitivity; nevertheless, residual MXene in the system frequently affects the sensitivity and detection range of the sensors, and its separation has also become an issue that has to be resolved.

Magnetic microspheres are frequently utilized as carriers in in-vitro diagnostic procedures, such as chemiluminescence [15] and liquid chip [16], because of their magnetic separation and functionalized surface. Magnetic microspheres have shown promise recently in the reduction of luminous background on intrinsic magnetism and in the isolation of residues [17]. Using magnetic Fe3O4@GO to reduce background, a novel fluorescent aptamer sensor for sensitive pesticide identification was effectively constructed [18]. Consequently, developing a magnetic composite material that combines AgNPs and MXene could offer a fresh approach to highly sensitive and efficient fluorescence detection.

Here, we build highly sensitive biosensors for tumor indicators by utilizing MXene and magnetic microspheres. Initially, MXene was self-assembled to cover magnetic microspheres as energy acceptor and AgNPs were generated in situ on MXene to improve quenching performance. The ability to detect NSE was then examined when NSE antibodies coupled with carbon dots which act as energy donor were adsorbed on microspheres to create biosensors. Moreover, distinct fluoresce probes were used to create dual biosensors that could detect CEA and NSE simultaneously and showed exceptional performance analysis.

Styrene (St), divinyl benzene (DVB), methyl methacrylate (MMA), AgNO3, Triton X-305, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), PAH and phosphate buffer solution (1x) were purchased from Sigma. Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), benzoyl peroxide (BPO), dibutyl phthalate (DBP), toluene, ethanol, 2,2′-Azodi (2-methylbutyronitrile) (AMBN), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (NHS) were obtained from J&KScientific Ltd. Amino CQDs and CdSe/ZnS-630nm (QDs) were bought from Suzhou Xingshuo nano technology Co., Ltd. NSE, NSE antibody, CEA and CEA antibody were available from Univ Biotechnology Co., Ltd. FeCl2 was got from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nano-sized MXene was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Technology Co.,Ltd. All reagents were used without purification.

Morphological analysis of MXene, and Ag@MXene@MCMs nanocomposite were obtained using a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM; FEI Quanta 400 FEG America FEI) and transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI Talos F200X American FEI). Phase and crystallinity behavior of synthesized MXene, AgNPs and Ag@MXene @MCMs nanocomposite were determined in the range of 5°–80° using X-ray diffraction (XRD, SmartLab SE Japan Rigaku) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U–3900H) was utilized to record the absorbance spectra of amino-CQDs, MXene and Ag@MXene @MCMs nanohybrid. The thermal decomposition of the microspheres was characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, EXSTAR 6300, America) instrument in a nitrogen atmosphere in the temperature range of 50–800 °C (with a heating rate of 10.0 K/min). Magnetic measurements of the magnetic microspheres were carried out on an alternate gradient magnetometer (Micro Mag TM-2900, Shanghai Yihong Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) at room temperature. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area values of the microspheres were determined using an accelerated surface area and porosimetry analyzer (ASAP, Micromeritics 2020，America). The zeta potential measurements were collected from the Malvern, Nano ZS 90 Zetasizer. F-4600 FL Spectrophotometer was employed for fluorescence detection analysis.

Magnetic carboxyl microspheres (MCMs) were prepared through our previous work. First, suitable polystyrene seeds (support information) were prepared according to the work of Lv [19]. Next, porous microspheres, porous carboxyl microspheres and magnetic carboxyl microspheres were successively prepared based on the literature [20].

MXene@MCMs were prepared by self-assembly of PAH and MXene. Briefly, 1 mL PAH (0.1 M) and 5 mg MCMs were mixed thoroughly for 30 min, then separated by magnet and washed with deionized water. Afterwards, 1 mL Nano-sized MXene was added directly, stirring for several hours. The particles were collected by magnet and washed with deionized water. Repeating the above actions three times, MXene was successfully coated on the MCMs to obtain MXene@MCMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In suit synthesis of Ag@MXene@MCMs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Ag@MXene@MCMs have been obtained via one spot direct reduction of AgNO3 salt to Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) onto MXene@MCMs [21], In which the MXene nanosheets acted both as reducing agent and supporting matrix for AgNPs. 5 mg MXene@MCMs in 1 mL water were added into 10 mL of 1 mmol/L AgNO3 solution, stirring at room temperature for 12 h. Ag@MXene@MCMs were separated by magnet, and washed with deionized water three times.

To fabricate fluorescent anti-NSE/CQDs probe, amino CQDs were employed as light-emitting part. The conjugation was carried out by standard EDC/NHS coupling chemistry [22]. Briefly, 19.1 mg EDC and 21.7 mg NHS are dissolved in PBS respectively. 4 μL anti-NSE (5.2 mg/mL), 100 μL EDC and 100 μL NHS were incubated together to activate the carboxyl of anti-NSE. After that, excessive amino CQDs were added to the above solution to conjugate with anti-NSE. Ag@MXene@MCMs (1 mg) were mixed with anti-NSE/CQDs, shaking at 1000 rpm for 12 h to prepare anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs composite. Finally, anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs were collected by magnet and washed with deionized water to remove excessive CQDs and anti-NSE/CQDs. Similar processes were carried out to prepare anti-CEA/QDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs.

The detection of target cancer biomarker NSE and CEA were carried out in PBS at room temperature by recording the fluorescence emission spectra. In particular, 1 mg anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs (or anti-CEA/QDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs) were mixed with different concentration (0.0001–1500 ng/mL) of NSE (or CEA) antigen, and allowed to incubate for 30 min. Consequently, the alterations of fluorescence intensity were computed at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm with the increasing NSE antigen concentration in the resultant mixture. The selectivity studies of the fabricated biosensor have been performed by adding fixed concentration (50 μL, 0.1 ng/mL) of various interfering biomarkers into anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs solution, followed by the measurement of corresponding emission spectra. Furthermore, dual biomarker detection of NSE and CEA was investigated simultaneously.

The serum of real samples was obtained through the following steps: (1) Collecting blood samples and placing them in test tubes without anticoagulants; (2) natural agglutination at room temperature for 30–60 min, until blood is coagulated; (3) centrifuge at speed of 2000–3000 rpm for 5–10 min, and the supernatant is serum.

1 mg anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs or/and anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs were mixed with different concentrations (150-10 ng/mL) in a gradient manner. The fluorescence value was obtained, and the corresponding concentration (ys) was calculated accordingly. The calculated concentration of the measured fluorescence value of 10 % serum was recorded as the background concentration (y0), and the actually added concentration was recorded as yi. According to the above three concentrations, we evaluated the application of the detection probe in simulating the actual environment detection.

The mechanism of our proposed anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs biosensor for detecting tumor maker NSE is shown in Fig. 1 , which could be mainly divided into three steps. In the first place, micron-sized polystyrene carboxyl porous magnetic microspheres with uniform size were prepared by seed polymerization and in situ participation of Fe3O4. In order to coat MXene onto MCMs, PAH was employed as medium to modify the surface of MCMs from negative to positive, which was beneficial to further coating, and MXene@MCMs were successfully obtained by self-assembly. By the reducibility of MXene, silver nanoparticles were grown in situ on the MXene@MCMs to obtain Ag@MXene@MCMs composites. Secondly, anti-NSE/CQDs was adsorbed onto the surface of Ag@MXene@MCMs by means of physical adsorption between antibody and Ag, where appeared abrupt fluorescence quenching due to dipole-surface interactions and energy transfer. With the addition of antigen NSE, anti-NSE/CQDs fell off from Ag@MXene@MCMs, and the fluorescence recovered. The recovery of fluorescence value depended on the amounts of added NSE.

The magnetic carboxyl microspheres were prepared by three procedure, dispersion polymerization, seed polymerization and in situ participation. As shown in Fig. S1A, the single-layered MXene nanosheets were obtained. The SEM images of seed microspheres, porous carboxyl microspheres and MCMs are shown in Fig. S1 B-D. The MCMs were uniform and monodisperse, and the diameter was about 6 μm. As the preparation of MCMs had been reported in our previous work [20], it will not be discussed here. Due to the negative surface of MCMs and MXene, the positive PAH was employed as medium to self-assembly. Compared with Fig. 2 A and B, the pore on the microspheres disappeared and the surface of MXene@MCMs was rough. The coating was carried out three times to obtain thorough coverage surface of MXene. When MXene was coated only once, the porous structure on the surface was still visible (Fig. S2A). The more times coating was carried out, the fewer pores were found on the surface of microspheres, which are shown in Fig. S2B-D. At the same time, Zeta potential was also achieved for characterization of self-assembly in Fig. S3. The potential changed greatly before and after each layer of self-assembly, and the overall potential of microspheres showed a downward trend after three times of self-assembly. Moreover, as BET curve is shown in Fig. S4, N2 uptake amount decreased from 200 cm3/g before coating to 50 cm3/g after coating. All characterization proved that MXene was successfully covered on the microspheres.

Because of the excellent reducibility of MXene [23], Ag+ was directly in situ to silver nanoparticles on the surface of MXene@MCMs. The SEM image of Ag@MXene@MCMs was shown in Fig. 2C, and Ag particles were evenly distributed on the microspheres. The EDS of Ag@MXene@MCMs was used to analyze the elemental spectrum (Fig. 2D–I). From the analysis of surface scanning data, the shape of C, Ti, and O are spherical, indicating that MXene is uniformly distributed and wrapped on the surface of microspheres. Meanwhile, Fig. S5 shows the atom percentage of different elements by EDS spectrum. The result presents that Ag nanoparticles’ atom percentage is 0.79 %, which was beneficial for following antibody conjugation.

The structural characteristics of MXene, Ag@ MXene@MCMs nano-hybrids and silver nanoparticles were studied by XRD (Fig. 3 A). The XRD pattern of Ag@MXene@MCMs not only displayed the diffraction peaks of MXene ((002), 008)), but also showed the diffraction peaks of AgNPs (PDF#04–0783, (111), (200), (220), (311)), which indicated the formation of Ag@MXene@MCMs composites. Compared with the original MXene, the position of the characteristic peak after recombination has a certain deviation, and we guess that the peak intensity of AgNPs is too high to cover up the original peak.

Fig. 3B shows UV–Vis absorption of AgNPs, MXene and Ag@MXene@MCMs. It can be seen that AgNPs has a sharp peak at 460 nm, and MXene has a broad peak at 300–500 nm, which are consistent with the previous literature reports [24]. Meanwhile, there are two absorption peaks at 300 nm and 460 nm in the Ag@MX@MCMs complex, which proved the successful preparation of Ag@MX@MCMs [12]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3C, the MCMs and Ag@MXene@MCMs have a superparamagnetic response and their saturation magnetization were 2.78 emu g−1 and 1.16 emu g−1, respectively. The results indicate that the magnetization decreases with the load of Mxene and AgNPs. However, this does not affect the magnetic separation performance during detection. The successful preparation of Ag@MXene@MCMs could be also proved by weight loss in TGA analysis (Fig. 3D). The TGA curves demonstrated that the weight loss of MCMs was 97.85 %, while the weight loss of MXene@MCMs and Ag@MXene@MCMs was 53.22 % and 50.55 % at 700 °C, which indicated that the weight ratio of the MXene/PAH and AgNPs was 44.63 % and 2.67 %.

The excitation wavelength could influence the intensity of emission fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs at different excitation are shown in Fig. 4 A, and the best excitation wavelength is 350 nm which is used in the following experiments. In order to obtain anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs, antibody anti-NSE was conjugated with amino CQDs firstly, and then incubated with Ag@MXene@MCMs. Compared to PBS coupling buffer with MES as shown in Fig. 4B, it is obvious that PBS buffer is more suitable for coupling. In previous reports [25,26], the activation temperature of EDC/NHS had a great influence on the coupling efficiency, and the temperature of 25 °C and 4 °C was used for the coupling process as in previous research. The intensity of fluorescence recovery of coupling at 25 °C is higher than that at 4 °C (Fig. 4C), so the coupling efficiency at 25 °C is better. According to previous reports [11,27], ultracentrifugation or dialysis bags are usually used to remove excess CQDs or antibodies for purification based on molecular weight. However, removing excess CQDs or antibodies using these two methods will lose a lot of required products. Furthermore, it is difficult to be carried out and time-consuming. Therefore, an effective method was developed for coupling and surface absorption of anti-CQDs on Ag@MXene@MCMs. Considering that precious metals can adsorb antibodies but not CQDs, excessive CQDs existed in the coupling solution, which ensured every antibody could conjugate with CQDs. When Ag@MXene@MCMs were added to the coupling solution, anti-NSE/CQDs were absorbed on Ag@MXene@MCMs and redundant CQDs were removed by multiple separation and washing. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescent intensity of the supernate falls to near zero after 4 times washing.

Proper amounts of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs were very important to detection system. As shown in Fig. S6, with the amounts of microspheres increasing from 0.2 mg to 1.0 mg gradually, the fluorescence intensity released by antigen gradually increased. Because with the increase of the amounts of microspheres, the more sites that can adsorb antibodies, the stronger the fluorescence intensity was. When the amounts of microspheres are increased to 1.2 mg, the intensity of fluorescence release remains unchanged, which means that adding microspheres will not further increase the fluorescence intensity. So, the best amounts of microspheres were 1.0 mg.

The best preparation time of anti-NSE/CQDs and Ag@MXene@MCMs was also investigated. Anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs biosensors were prepared at different reaction time (3–18 h), and then the biosensors were incubated with NSE for 30 min. It was obvious that the Ag@MXene@MCMs prepared at more than 12 h the fluorescence recovery was relatively unchanged shown in Fig. 4E. The optimization of the detection time of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs biosensors was carried out. As shown in Fig. 4F, the fluorescence intensity increased as the incubation time of NSE antigen and anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs longer, and the fluorescence value remained relatively stable after 30 min, indicating that the best detection time was 30 min as sample added. We optimized the experimental conditions and obtained the best results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs based fluorescent biosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
With the increase of NSE antigen concentration, the fluorescence recovery intensity also increased (Fig. 5 A–B), which was attributed to the formation of specific complex structure between antigen and antibody. While the binding force of antigen and antibody was strong, the anti-NSE/CQDs fell off from the silver surface and the fluorescence intensity recovered. Meanwhile, further experiments were carried out for verification of fluorescence recovery. The fluorescence response of the CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs has been recorded as NSE addition. The findings obtained (Fig. S7) reveal that there is no substantial restoration of the fluorescence intensity against the added NSE concentration, suggesting that the restoration of fluorescence intensity is primarily due to the unique antibody-antigen interactions. In Fig. 5C the range of linear correlation linear regression description obtained in the dynamic process is 0.0001–1500 ng/mL Equation Eq. (1.1). Then we calculate LOD = 0.03 pg/mL.
Eq. (1.1)
I = 740
LOG 10
NSE
( ng / mL )
+ 3691.27

Similar to the system of in-situ growth of silver particles on the surface, we added the corresponding concentration of antigen in anti-NSE/CQDs/MXene@MCMs according to the gradient, and the fluorescence recovery was shown in Fig. 5D and E. Based on plotting the fluorescence recovery value at 440 nm, the relationship between the logarithmic value of concentration and the fluorescence recovery value was obtained (Fig. 5F). The range of linear correlation linear regression description obtained in the dynamic process is 50–1000 ng/mL Equation Eq. (1.2).
Eq. (1.2)
I = 1437
LOG 10
NSE
( ng / mL )
−
480.11

Obviously, the points in the scatter plot are a curve, and the linear correlation was relatively low. We selected several most relevant points for fitting, and R2 = 0.98 was not as good as the silver particle probe (Fig. 5F). By comparison, we found that the biosensor platform loaded with Ag exhibited better LOD, wider linear detection range, higher sensitivity and faster response time. We suspect that two factors were mainly taken into account: first, there were thiol groups on the antibody molecule, allowing antibody to interact strongly between the AgNPs. At the same time, the MXene offer large surface area for loading dense AgNPs, which benefit well for immobilizing more antibodies, enhancing the sensitivity and detection range [28,29]. Moreover, the quick and effective isolation of residues can reduce the luminescent background by the magnetism of Ag@MXene@MCMs.

Furthermore, in order to exhibit the excellent performance of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs biosensors, previously reported technology for NSE detection were summarized in Table 1 . It is clear that the LOD of the biosensors prepared in this paper is much lower than that of most reported probes, and the detection range is also much broader than others. We speculate that the specific surface area of microspheres and the introduction of silver particles make the detection limit far lower than that of the reported probes.

The developed fluorescent biosensor has been found to exhibit good performance toward the detection of NSE biomarker. However, in human serum, various interfering biomarkers (other than NSE) such as Carbohydrate antigen19-9 (CA19-9)， pepsinogen (PGI/II)， CEA is also present [36]. Therefore, in the presence of these potential serum biomarkers, it is very important to check the selectivity of the developed biosensor. Fig. 6 A shows the response of the biosensor in the presence of different biomarkers (50 μL, 1 ng/mL). It has been observed that the fluorescence recovery of interfering cancer biomarkers is as weak as blank, while NSE antigen will lead to strong fluorescence recovery. These findings clearly show that the fluorescence recovery is caused by the interaction between NSE biomarker and specific antibody of NSE on biosensors, which proves the excellent selectivity of the proposed fluorescence biosensor for NSE detection.

In addition, the fluorescence immunosensor also needs to ensure good repeatability of experimental results, that is, reproducibility. So, five batches of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs were prepared under the same conditions, and five batches of biosensors were used to detect a specific concentration of NSE antigen (1 ng/mL). According to the results, the fluorescence recovery values of the five tests are similar, and the relative deviation is less than 5 %, which proved that the biosensor has good repeatability. We kept the anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs at 4 °C for 2 months and then added 1500 ng/mL antigen. The fluorescence intensity only decreased by 6.5% compared to the initial value, indicating the probe's excellent stability (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual biosensors for simultaneous detection of NSE and CEA
ce:section.ce:para: In practical use, simultaneous detection of multiple tumor markers is significant for cancer diagnosis since most of cancers have several markers. CEA as a normal tumor marker was employed to evaluate the performance of dual detection with NSE. Followed by the preparation of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs, the anti-NSE and CQDs were replaced by anti-CEA and QDs (626 nm) respectively, and anti-CEA/QDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs were successfully obtained. Before constructing a dual detection system, we first verified that CEA antibody only changed the types of quantum dots and made them emit at different wavelengths under the same probe, and then obtained the fluorescence recovery diagram for detecting CEA antibody only, and the corresponding detection limit (0.9 pg/mL) was obtained according to the fluorescence recovery diagram (Fig. S8A-C).

From Fig. 7 A, it can be observed that when two different antigens, CEA and NSE, are added into the same testing system in a concentration gradient, the fluorescence recovers at 456 nm and 626 nm respectively. Importantly, the fluorescence signals do not interfere with each other, indicating that the emission spectra of the two quantum dots do not overlap. The strong linear relationship (R2 > 0.99) is shown in Fig. 7B. This suggests that the fluorescence probes not only can detect NSE but also simultaneously detect CEA.

In order to perform testing of actual samples, the recovery experiment was carried out by adding standard samples (50 μL NSE antigen (10, 20, 50, 100 and 150 ng/mL)) in healthy human serum. Fig. S9 shows the results of the proposed immunosensor (anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs) on five different serum samples doped with NSE. Using the equation to determine the recovery (%) in the spiked serum sample, the results were listed in Table S1.
Eq. (1.3)
% R e c o v e r y =
y i
−
y 0
y s
× 100
Where ys is the actual NSE spiked concentration and yi, yo are the obtained concentrations in spiked and unspiked samples, respectively. The anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs based fluorescent immunosensor exhibit an average recovery of ∼96%. The obtained results signify that the fabricated immunosensor can quantitatively detect the NSE biomarker in the clinical samples. Similar evaluation of CEA in real samples was shown in Table S2, which also maintains a good fluorescence recovery rate, which shows the practicability of the real sample system.

This work successfully produced a fluorescent biosensor of Ag@MXene@MCMs/CQDs/anti-NSE, which was employed for extremely sensitive tumor marker NSE detection. Ag nanoparticles were coated on the MXene by in situ reduction, and MXene was uniformly and totally on the surface of MCMs by self-assembly during the preparation of anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs. In comparison to earlier reports, the anti-NSE/CQDs/Ag@MXene@MCMs composites biosensors shown notable advantages in terms of their superior performance in detecting NSE, such as a broader linear range (0.0001–1500 ng/mL), an exceedingly low limit of detection (0.03 pg/mL), and convenient usability. Additionally, the suggested biosensor approach performed exceptionally well in CEA detection. This means that it might be used in clinical settings for various indicators as long as the antibody is changed. Furthermore, we successfully confirmed that this probe could be used for double detection (CEA, NSE), which significantly increased its practicability. Additionally, we believe that the technique may be applied to more markers at the same time.

Chenyu Yin: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiang Zhao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhizhou Liu: Writing – review & editing. Le Ma: Visualization, Data curation. Pengyun Sun: Formal analysis. Liang He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Huajie Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Pengli Bai: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China (Grant No. 2022YFC2406600), Youth Foundation of Shandong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. ZR2020QB110), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association, the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. 2019321).
